title: China Growth Misses Target, Senate Blocks Defence Bill Over Iran Provisions, US Consumer Prices Drop Sharly
subhead: China's Q2 GDP slips to 4.3%, Senate Democrats sink the defence authorisation over war powers, and US inflation posts its biggest monthly drop in years — but the reprieve may not last as oil surges on Hormuz disruptions.

sections.heading: China Growth Slows to 4.3%, Missing Beijing's Target
sections.body:
China's economy grew at 4.3% in the second quarter of 2026, well below the government's official target of around 5%, according to BBC. The slowdown was driven by weak domestic demand and the knock-on effects of the Iran war on global oil prices, which have weighed on Chinese manufacturing costs and consumer confidence. Exports remained a relative bright spot, but not enough to offset the drag from property, retail, and industrial investment.

The data adds pressure on Beijing to deploy further stimulus. The People's Bank of China has already cut rates twice this year, but the property sector — long the engine of Chinese household wealth — remains in deep contraction. With Hormuz disruptions pushing energy costs higher for import-dependent economies, China's recovery path looks increasingly uncertain.
sections.whyItMatters: The world's second-largest economy is losing steam at a moment when global trade routes are under strain. A weaker China dampens demand for commodities and manufactured goods alike, complicating the growth outlook for emerging markets that depend on Chinese investment.

sections.heading: Senate Democrats Block Defence Bill Over Iran War, Israel Provisions
sections.body:
US Senate Democrats voted down the annual National Defence Authorisation Act on Monday, defeating the $886bn military spending bill 50-46, according to Al Jazeera. The blockade came over provisions that would restrict congressional war powers regarding the Iran conflict and mandate continued military support to Israel — measures Democrats argued gave the executive branch a blank cheque for an open-ended war.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune had pushed for a swift vote before the August recess, but Democrats unified around the objection that the bill failed to rein in presidential authority over the Iran campaign. The vote throws the Pentagon's budget planning into uncertainty and signals that congressional Democrats are willing to leverage must-pass legislation to force a debate over the scope of US involvement in the Gulf.
sections.whyItMatters: The first significant congressional revolt over the Iran war. If Democrats hold the line, it could force the White House to negotiate war-power constraints — or face a funding gap for the military.

sections.heading: US Consumer Prices Drop 0.4% in June — But the Reprieve May Be Fleeting
sections.body:
US consumer prices fell 0.4% in June, the sharpest monthly drop in years, driven by a 9.5% plunge in petrol prices and a 5.7% fall in energy costs overall, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported, according to Al Jazeera. Annual inflation eased to 3.5% from 4.2% in May. But analysts warn the June data is already stale: oil prices have surged back above $80/bbl for WTI and near $84 for Brent since the Hormuz escalation reignited, and petrol averages are climbing again.

GasBuddy's Patrick De Haan noted that petrol prices have already risen from $3.79 to $3.85 per gallon and are heading toward $4, with diesel approaching $5. CME FedWatch shows an 87.7% probability the Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75% this month, with the remaining probability on a 25bp hike. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh told lawmakers the central bank has 'no tolerance for persistently elevated inflation'.
sections.whyItMatters: The June CPI drop is real but backward-looking. With Hormuz disruptions pushing oil higher again, July's inflation print will likely reverse the gains — keeping rate cuts off the table and squeezing consumers.

sections.heading: Gulf Escalation: Tankers Hit, Hormuz Tolls Imposed, Yemen Reignites
sections.body:
Trump announced a 20% charge on all cargo transiting the Strait of Hormuz and a reimposition of the US naval blockade on Iranian ports, effective Tuesday, according to BBC. The UAE accused Iran of a 'brazen' attack on two national tankers that killed one Indian crew member and wounded eight others — four seriously. Iran's IRGC confirmed it struck the vessels, claiming they had ignored warnings and attempted to transit a mined route. Iran also targeted US military assets in Kuwait, weapons depots in Bahrain, and an air base in Jordan with ballistic missiles, Iranian state media reported.

Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi pushed back on Trump's 'GUARDIAN' framing, posting that Iran 'has always been the GUARDIAN of the Strait and will remain so FOREVER,' adding that 20% is 'too much' and Tehran would 'be fair.' The International Maritime Organization stated there is 'no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait.'

Separately, Yemen's Houthis launched missiles at Abha airport in Saudi Arabia after strikes on Sanaa's airport — the most significant escalation in the largely dormant Saudi-Houthi conflict since a 2022 truce. The internationally recognised Yemeni government said it struck the Sanaa runway to prevent an Iranian plane from landing; the Houthis warned airlines to avoid Saudi airspace.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz toll and tanker strikes mark a new phase: commercial shipping is now directly targeted by both sides, and a second front has opened with the Houthis. The legal basis for tolls is contested, and energy markets are responding in real time.

sections.heading: Sudan Sentences RSF Leader Hemedti to Death; El Niño Threatens East Africa and Asia
sections.body:
A Sudanese court in Port Sudan sentenced Rapid Support Forces commander Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, known as Hemedti, to death in absentia for war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide related to atrocities in West Darfur, Al Jazeera reports. Fifteen other senior RSF figures received the same sentence. The ruling is the first conviction of top RSF leaders since the civil war began, but its practical impact is limited — Hemedti's whereabouts are unknown and the RSF controls much of western Sudan, including Darfur and el-Fasher.

A UN fact-finding mission concluded last week that the RSF committed genocide during its siege and capture of el-Fasher, citing mass killings, gang rapes, and deliberate starvation of civilians. The UK and more than two dozen states warn that 500,000 civilians around el-Obeid face the risk of large-scale atrocities as the RSF masses forces around the city.

Meanwhile, the International Rescue Committee warned that a record-strength El Niño threatens severe flooding and famine from Somalia to Pakistan. The US Climate Prediction Center assessed an 81% chance of this becoming one of the most powerful El Niño events since 1950, peaking October-December. In Bangladesh, 15 Rohingya refugees have been killed and 10,000 displaced by landslides and flooding in Cox's Bazar. The World Bank warned rice yields could fall 20-50% across the hardest-hit parts of South Asia and East Africa.
sections.whyItMatters: Two slow-burn crises converging: Sudan's judicial process can't reach those in power on the ground, and a record El Niño will compound food insecurity in regions already strained by conflict and Hormuz-driven fertiliser cost increases.

sections.heading: Around the World: Ryanair Decompression, Bangkok Fire Toll, Cuba's Third Blackout, Indonesia Jails Gojek Founder
sections.body:
A Ryanair flight from Thessaloniki to Memmingen suffered a catastrophic cabin window failure shortly after takeoff on Friday, nearly sucking a 61-year-old passenger head-first out of the aircraft. His wife and two other passengers held him for nearly two minutes before pulling him back inside. The man, Ljubisa Karovic, remains hospitalised with serious hand injuries and burns. The 18-year-old Boeing 737-800 was operated by Malta Air; multiple aviation authorities are investigating. BBC reports the flight dropped 9,000 feet before returning to Thessaloniki.

Thailand's Bangkok bar fire death toll has risen to 30, with investigators finding locked doors and a lack of emergency exit signage. BBC reports that survivors described patrons running through flames to escape. In Cuba, the power grid collapsed for the third time in 10 days, according to Al Jazeera, underscoring the island's deepening energy crisis. Indonesia's conviction of Gojek co-founder Nadiem Makarim to 10 years for corruption in a school laptop procurement case has raised investor confidence fears — the CSIS in Jakarta warned that 'non-economic factors, such as legal certainty and the quality of the judicial system, are absolute prerequisites' for foreign capital. US and Russian astronauts launched into orbit on a joint Soyuz mission — a rare pocket of cooperation between the two governments. And UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer became the first serving British PM to receive France's Légion d'honneur, awarded by President Macron at a Coalition of the Willing meeting in Paris.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ryanair incident raises urgent questions about Boeing 737 aging-fleet maintenance. Bangkok's fire toll highlights regulatory failures in entertainment venues across Southeast Asia. Indonesia's prosecution of a tech founder-turned-minister signals political risk for foreign investors under the Prabowo administration.

bottomLine: China's slowdown, the Senate's Iran-war revolt, and a fleeting US inflation drop frame a day where economic indicators mask the instability underneath. The Gulf conflict escalated again — tanker strikes, a contested Hormuz toll, and Houthi missiles on Saudi Arabia — while Sudan's symbolic death sentence for Hemedti and a record-strength El Niño compound the humanitarian outlook. The Iran-US conflict is no longer just a military story; it is reshaping congressional politics, inflation expectations, and global food security in real time.
